tools.name: future_value
tools.description: Calculate future value of an investment with compound interest. Use this for investment growth projections, savings account growth, and retirement planning. For comparing multiple rates and periods, use future_value_table instead.

rate.description: Annual interest rate as decimal (e.g., 0.05 for 5%)

years.description: Number of years for the investment

tools.name: present_value
tools.description: Calculate present value (current worth) of a future amount. Use this for loan valuation, bond pricing, and investment analysis. For comparing multiple scenarios, use future_value_table with different periods and rates instead.

rate.description: Annual discount rate as decimal (e.g., 0.05 for 5%)

years.description: Number of years until the amount is received

tools.name: discount
tools.description: Calculate final price after applying a percentage discount. Use this for retail pricing, sale calculations, and cost savings analysis. For comparing multiple discount percentages, use discount_table instead.

discount.description: Discount percentage (e.g., 20 for 20% or 0.20 as decimal, depending on input format)

tools.name: markup
tools.description: Calculate selling price from cost and markup percentage. Use this for product pricing, profit margin calculation, and business pricing strategy. For comparing multiple markup scenarios, use discount_table which also shows profit margins.

markup.description: Markup percentage (e.g., 30 for 30% or 0.30 as decimal, depending on input format)

tools.name: future_value_table
tools.description: Generate a comparison table of future values across multiple interest rates and time periods. Use this for investment scenario comparison, rate shopping, and long-term planning. For single calculations, use future_value instead.

periods.description: Time periods in years to compare (e.g., [1, 5, 10, 20])

tools.name: discount_table
tools.description: Compare multiple discount percentages for the same price. Use this for bulk pricing strategies, promotional planning, and price comparison. For single discount calculations, use discount instead.

tools.name: launch_ui
tools.description: Launch the interactive web UI for the financial calculator. Use this for visual, tab-based calculations with real-time results and beautiful gradient UI. For command-line calculations, use the individual calculation tools instead.

port.description: Port number for the web server (default: 5050)

messages.content: Hey, I just got my first book advance and I'm kind of curious - if I don't touch it and let it sit there, how much could it grow over time with compound interest? Like if I got $50,000 or something, what would that look like in 10 or 20 years? I've never really thought about this kind of thing before.

Congratulations on the book advance! That's exciting. Let me show you what $50,000 could grow to over 10 and 20 years at a few different interest rates - that way you can see the range of possibilities.

That's a nice chunk of change to start with! Here's what $50,000 could grow to:

**After 10 years:**
- At a conservative 3% (like a savings account): **$67,195** — about $17K growth
- At a moderate 5% (typical stock market average): **$81,445** — more than $31K gained
- At a higher 7%: **$98,358** — almost doubling your money

**After 20 years:**
- At 3%: **$90,306**
- At 5%: **$132,665** — more than 2.5x your original
- At 7%: **$193,929** — almost 4x!

A few quick notes since you're new to this:
- **Compound interest** means you earn interest on your interest — that's what makes it grow faster over time
- The stock market has historically averaged around 7-10% per year over long periods, but it can go up and down
- Savings accounts are safer but currently pay more like 3-5%
- If you wanted to be really conservative, you could also look at Treasury bonds or CDs

Would you like me to run any other scenarios? For example, I could show you what happens if you add a little money each year, or look at different amounts.

Oh that's really helpful, thanks! I'm curious though - what if I wanted to see it grow even longer, like 30 or 40 years? And also maybe what would happen if I added a little bit to it each year from my royalties - like maybe $5,000 extra per year? I know that's a lot to save but I'm just curious how that would compare to just letting the initial amount sit there.
Great questions! Let me show you the longer timeframes first, and then I'll address the annual contributions part.

Now for the longer terms:

**After 30 years:**
- At 3%: **$121,363**
- At 5%: **$216,097** — over 4x your original
- At 7%: **$380,613** — more than 7.5x!

**After 40 years:**
- At 3%: **$163,102**
- At 5%: **$351,999** — 7x your original
- At 7%: **$748,723** — nearly 15x!

---

Now, for your question about adding $5,000/year — that's a great question, but I have to be upfront with you: my current calculator tools don't have a feature that lets me model *annual contributions* on top of the initial principal. It's a bit beyond what I can compute here.

But here's the thing — if you're adding $5,000 per year from royalties, you're actually putting in a *lot* more money over time:
- Over 20 years: $100,000 in contributions
- Over 30 years: $150,000 in contributions

So the comparison isn't really fair! You'd be comparing $50,000 sitting there vs. $50,000 + $100,000-$150,000 in total contributions. The power of compound interest on those additional contributions would be enormous — you'd likely end up with significantly more than the "just the initial $50K" scenario.

If you'd like, I could explain how you'd calculate that manually, or if you want to explore other scenarios like "what if I only contributed for the first 10 years?" — happy to think through that with you!

Oh that's really interesting actually! Now I'm thinking about other ways to use my book earnings - like if I wanted to create some merchandise related to my books. Like t-shirts or bookmarks or something with my characters on them. How would I even figure out what to charge for something like that? I know I need to cover costs and make some profit, but I've never thought about how pricing works for stuff like that.

That's a fun question! There's actually a simple concept called **markup** that helps you figure this out. It's basically: take your cost to make the item, then add a percentage on top for profit.

Let me show you how it works with some example costs for typical merchandise:
{"status": "success", "tool": "markup", "cost": 8.0, "markup": 50.0, "selling_price": 12.0, "profit": 4.0}
messages.name: skill-tools-markup

messages.content: {"status": "success", "tool": "markup", "cost": 0.5, "markup": 100.0, "selling_price": 1.0, "profit": 0.5}
messages.name: skill-tools-markup

messages.content:
Here's how markup works:

**T-shirt example (cost: $8 to make):**
- 50% markup → sell for **$12** (profit: $4)
- 100% markup → sell for **$16** (profit: $8)

**Bookmark example (cost: $0.50 to make):**
- 100% markup → sell for **$1.00** (profit: $0.50)

The markup percentage is basically how much extra you add for profit. A few things to think about:

**Common markup ranges:**
- 50% is pretty standard for retail — you cover costs and have a reasonable profit
- 100% (doubling your money) is more typical for specialty or handcrafted items
- Some products can handle 200%+ (like branded items with collector appeal!)

**Other factors to consider:**
- **Platform fees** — if you sell on Etsy, Amazon, etc., they take a cut (often 10-15%)
- **Shipping/packaging** — don't forget to factor in envelopes, bubble mailers, etc.
- **Volume discounts** — you might offer lower prices for bundles (like "3 bookmarks for $2.50")

For book merch specifically, fans often happily pay a premium because it's *your* book — so you might be able to charge more than generic merchandise would fetch.

Would you like to play around with some specific costs you have in mind? I can run the numbers for you!

Oh that's really helpful! Now I'm thinking about another scenario - like when I want to run a sale or promotion. Like if I wanted to offer a discount on my merchandise, how do different discount strategies work? Like what's the difference between saying "20% off" versus "buy one get one half off" or something like that? Which one actually gives customers a better deal, and which one is better for me?
Great question! This is something a lot of creators struggle with. Let me run some concrete numbers for you to show the difference.
{"status": "success", "tool": "discount", "original_price": 16.0, "discount_percent": 20.0, "final_price": 12.8, "savings": 3.2}
messages.name: skill-tools-discount

messages.content:
Let's compare some common discount approaches:

**Simple 20% off ($16 item):**
- You save: **$3.20**
- Customer pays: **$12.80**

Now let me show a "buy one get one half off" comparison — this works differently because it's based on buying two items:
{"status": "success", "tool": "discount", "final_price": 24.0, "savings": 8.0, "original_price": 32.0, "discount_percent": 25.0}
messages.name: skill-tools-discount

messages.content:
Here's how they compare:

**"20% off" a single $16 shirt:**
- Customer saves: **$3.20**
- Customer pays: **$12.80**

**"Buy one get one half off" (two $16 shirts):**
- Total original price: $32
- You pay: $24 (first shirt $16 + second shirt $8)
- Customer saves: **$8.00** (effectively 25% off the total!)

**So which is better for the customer?**
BOGO half off is actually a *slightly* better deal (25% total savings vs 20%), but here's the kicker: **it gets them to buy more**.

**Which is better for you?**
- **20% off:** Simpler, but customers might just buy one
- **BOGO half off:** You make slightly less per item ($12 each vs $12.80), but you sell *twice as much volume*

**A few other discount strategies to consider:**

| Strategy | Customer Savings | Seller Perspective |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| "Buy 2, get 1 free" | 33% off | Great for clearing inventory |
| Flash sale (limited time) | Creates urgency | Boosts quick sales |
| Bundle (3 for $10) | ~17% off per item | Predictable revenue |

For book merch specifically, BOGO deals can work really well — fans love grabbing extras for friends who also read your books!

Would you like me to run any specific scenarios?

That would be great! I've been thinking about all these different scenarios - the investment growth over time, how to price my merchandise, and those discount strategies. It would be really helpful to have some kind of visual calculator where I can play around with all of these at once and see everything laid out. Like being able to adjust the interest rates or the costs or the discount percentages and see how it changes things in real time. Does that kind of interactive tool exist? I'd love to try it out!

Yes, exactly! There's actually an interactive web calculator I can launch for you that does all of this — you can adjust sliders and inputs and see the results change in real time. It has separate tabs for each type of calculation:

- **Future Value** (for your investment growth)
- **Present Value** (the opposite - figuring out what you need to start with)
- **Discount** (for sale pricing)
- **Markup** (for merchandise pricing)

Let me fire it up for you!
{"status": "success", "tool": "launch_ui", "message": "Financial calculator UI launched successfully", "port": 5050, "url": "http://localhost:5050"}
messages.name: skill-tools-launch_ui

messages.content:
I've launched the interactive calculator for you! Here's what you can do:

**🌐 Access it at: http://localhost:5050**

It has a clean visual interface where you can:

- **Future Value tab** — Enter your $50,000, adjust the interest rate with a slider, and see how it grows over 10, 20, 30, or 40 years instantly
- **Discount tab** — Type in any price and any discount percentage to see the final sale price
- **Markup tab** — Enter your cost and markup % to see your selling price and profit

The calculator has a nice gradient UI with real-time results, so you can just drag sliders and watch the numbers change.

Go ahead and try it out — play around with different interest rates, see how adding even 1-2% makes a big difference over 30-40 years, or test out different markup percentages for your merchandise pricing! Let me know if you have questions after you've had a chance to try it.

Oh perfect, this looks exactly like what I was hoping for! I'm excited to try it out. Let me mess around with the interest rate sliders and see how my $50k grows at different rates - that should be really satisfying to watch change in real time. Thanks for launching this for me!

You're welcome! Have fun with it — there's something satisfying about watching compound interest work its magic with those sliders. 👍

Enjoy exploring, and if you have any other questions down the road (whether it's about pricing, investments, or anything else), feel free to come back. Good luck with the book and the merch!
